region_of_origin: Middle East (falafel origin); served as a Western-style bowl in Singapore
food_subgroup: Legume-based mixed bowl with grains and vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 covers falafel as a food item; the full energy bowl composite (grains, vegetables, sauce) is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers falafel; the composite bowl format including grains and salad components is not separately coded. Closest parent used for the dominant protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for falafel energy bowl. No code assigned.

main_ingredients: grain base (rice or quinoa or bulgur)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard ala carte energy bowl serving of approximately 380 g, comprising falafel patties, a grain base, mixed salad vegetables, and tahini-based dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Falafel, homemade / restaurant-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16057 and composite); supplemented with USDA Foundation Foods falafel entry (FDC ID 174289)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central falafel entries (FDC ID 174289, falafel, homemade) and a composite estimate for the full energy bowl including grain base (~80 g cooked rice or quinoa), mixed salad vegetables (~120 g), and tahini dressing (~30 g). The composite per-100 g values are weighted averages across the bowl components. This is an estimated composite; exact restaurant formulation is not available from an approved primary source. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Falafel and chickpea-based dishes — GI values from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; and University of Sydney GI Database chickpea/legume entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a falafel energy bowl composite. GI of 42 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 for chickpea-based dishes and legume patties (chickpeas GI ~28–36; falafel patties with grain base composite estimated ~40–45). A mid-point of 42 is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (42 × 81.7) / 100 ≈ 34.3, rounded to 34.2. Carbohydrate per serving = 21.5 g/100 g × 380 g = 81.7 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1178 mg per bowl serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full falafel energy bowl serving may contribute approximately 1178 mg sodium, representing roughly 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-sodium dressing are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~34 per serving) despite low GI of individual components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although falafel and chickpea components have a low glycemic index (~42), the large carbohydrate load from the grain base results in a moderate glycemic load per serving (~34); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion or substituting the grain base with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fat profile with predominantly unsaturated fats and zero cholesterol from plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The falafel energy bowl is plant-based with zero dietary cholesterol and a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (~5.6:1), supporting cardiovascular health; however, the deep-frying of falafel patties adds total fat and calories, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor overall energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Falafel Energy Bowl is a plant-based composed bowl dish served ala carte at select Singapore restaurants including Subway and mix-and-match concepts. It consists of deep-fried falafel patties made from ground chickpeas, herbs (parsley, coriander), garlic, and spices (cumin, coriander), served over a grain base such as rice, quinoa, or bulgur wheat, accompanied by fresh mixed salad vegetables including tomatoes, cucumbers, red onion, and leafy greens, and finished with a tahini-based dressing or hummus. Falafel originates from the Middle East and is one of the most widely consumed plant-based protein foods globally. The energy bowl format is a Western-influenced presentation popular in Singapore's health-conscious dining scene. Nutritionally, the bowl provides moderate energy (~185 kcal/100 g), meaningful plant protein (~6.8 g/100 g), dietary fibre (~3.4 g/100 g) from legumes and vegetables, and a favourable unsaturated fat profile from chickpeas and tahini. The dish is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. The glycemic index is low (~42) due to the legume base, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate due to the grain component. Clinically, the bowl is suitable for weight management and cardiovascular health but requires sodium monitoring due to dressing and seasoning contributions. It is a good source of folate, iron, and plant-based zinc.
common_user_queries: What is a falafel energy bowl?
How many calories in a falafel energy bowl?

confidence_reason: No exact match for a falafel energy bowl composite was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central falafel entries (FDC ID 174289) and a weighted composite estimate for the full bowl including grain base, salad vegetables, and tahini dressing. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database chickpea/legume analogues and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation and absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific restaurant product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central falafel (FDC ID 174289) weighted with cooked rice/quinoa (~80 g), mixed salad vegetables (~120 g), and tahini dressing (~30 g) components to produce per-100 g values for the full bowl (estimated total weight 380 g). 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for plant-based falafel and vegetable oil frying medium per USDA FoodData Central; naturally zero by product formulation. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as all ingredients are plant-derived. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are present in the standard formulation. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for the bowl components. 7. GI of 42 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 for chickpea-based dishes; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. 8. The input record listed cuisine as 'Sandwich' and food_group as 'Vegetables'; cuisine has been corrected to 'Middle Eastern / Western fusion bowl' and food_group retained as 'Vegetables' (legume-dominant). 9. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 10. Serving size of 380 g is estimated based on typical ala carte energy bowl portions at Singapore fast-casual restaurants; exact restaurant weight not confirmed from an approved source.
